topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a vibrant wave of joy washing over the ballroom. I clutched my lukewarm champagne flute so tightly my knuckles were white. Then, across the crowded dance floor, a familiar silhouette caught my eye. It was him. Liam. He was laughing, his arm draped casually around someone else's shoulder, the easy familiarity of their connection a physical blow. My breath hitched, a shallow, painful thing in my chest. Every muscle in my jaw tightened, a desperate attempt to keep my expression neutral, but the heat rising in my cheeks betrayed me. I took a deliberate step backward, bumping into a passing waiter, the small collision a necessary jolt to break the paralyzing stillness I was trying to maintain. I needed air, needed distance, but my feet felt rooted to the polished floor.

[story 2]

I managed a strained smile as I navigated the periphery of the reception, trying to appear engaged in the conversation happening two feet away. The air felt thick, heavy with forced pleasantries. When I saw him, standing near the buffet table, everything seemed to narrow. The sound of the string quartet faded into a dull hum. My stomach lurched, a cold, sickening twist. I focused intensely on the pattern of the expensive carpet beneath my shoes, counting the threads, anything to avoid making eye contact. When his gaze swept over me—a fleeting moment—I felt a sudden, sharp clenching in my throat. I excused myself abruptly, murmuring something about needing a refill, and retreated toward the relative sanctuary of the terrace, needing to feel the cool night air against my skin.

[story 3]

The noise level was overwhelming, a relentless barrage of celebratory chatter and clinking glasses. I was watching the proceedings from a shadowed corner, trying to observe without participating. Then, there he was. Liam. He looked different—more polished, undeniably happy—and the sight sent a sudden, unwelcome tremor through my core. I felt a distinct pressure behind my eyes, a blurring of the bright lights. My hands started to tremble almost imperceptibly, and I instinctively wrapped my arms around my own torso as if trying to hold myself together. When he glanced my way, a flicker of something unreadable crossed his face, and the brief exchange felt like an eternity stretched thin. I simply turned my back, focusing on the distant glow of the city lights, wishing the ground would swallow me whole.
